IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles a meaningful problem—extending standard word-representation methods to exploit ngram co-occurrence—and applies it across four widely used paradigms (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD). Empirical gains are substantive in some settings (e.g. bi-grams give ~8% improvement on Google analogy and ~10% on MSR for PPMI/analogy tasks) and the proposed efficient co-occurrence-matrix construction (enabling ‘bi bi’ on a laptop with ~12GB temp files) has practical value. However, benefits are inconsistent (little improvement on similarity tasks, SVD sometimes worse) and the authors themselves note sensitivity to hyperparameters, so the work is useful and likely to be built upon but not transformative for the field.

CLARITY.justification: The paper has a mostly logical structure (related work → model → experiments → conclusion) but the flow is frequently interrupted by repeated/parroting paragraphs and LaTeX/artifact noise, which makes reading choppy. Main contributions are stated near the end of the excerpt rather than clearly up front. Notation is generally introduced (e.g. T, W, C, θ in §3.1) but a few equations and definitions suffer from formatting issues that reduce readability. Figures/tables are cited and described (e.g. Fig.1/2, Tables 4–6) but are not shown here, and the text contains grammatical slips and awkward phrasing (e.g. "However, There", repeated sentences, "fancy task"), so proofreading and cleanup are needed.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The paper addresses core representation learning for NLP by introducing n-grams into SGNS, GloVe, PPMI and SVD (Section 3) and reports empirical results on standard similarity and analogy datasets used in Levy et al. (Section 4.1), comparing against canonical baselines (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD). It also proposes a practical matrix-construction strategy to reduce hardware costs (Sections 3/4.4), so the topic, audience, problem formulation, evaluation methodology, and positioning are all squarely within the scope and expectations of a top-tier NLP/ML venue.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper systematically extends existing embedding methods (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD) to include n-gram contexts (see Sec. 3.2 and 3.4) and proposes a memory-efficient co-occurrence matrix construction (noted in Experiments/Conclusion). However, similar ideas appear in prior work (Mikolov et al. 2013 phrases, Li et al. 2016 using n-gram embeddings, and prior use of n-grams in language models), so the core contribution is an incremental, practical extension and unification rather than a fundamentally new idea. The matrix-building trick provides some technical novelty, but overall the advance is moderate and would be informative but not surprising to experts.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper evaluates n-gram augmentation across four representation methods (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD) on standard similarity and analogy benchmarks (Levy et al. 2015 datasets) and reports detailed results (e.g. large analogy gains for PPMI/SGNS, minor GloVe gains, SVD failures) and proposes a memory-saving co-occurrence construction with concrete numbers. However, it lacks thorough ablation/sensitivity analyses (authors admit they did not tune hyperparameters or explore dynamic windows/weighting) and does not explore downstream tasks beyond similarity/analogy. Reproducibility is partly supported (implementation of algorithm 1 uploaded, some settings like low-frequency cutoff and use of clean strategy reported) but many training/hyperparameter details are not given. These strengths and gaps indicate solid experimental coverage but missing important analyses, so a 4/5 is appropriate.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against appropriate, standard baselines (SGNS/word2vec, GloVe, PPMI and SVD) and uses the same datasets/evaluation protocol as Levy et al. (2015) (sections 2 and 4.1), with explicit effort to keep contexts/windows comparable (Sec. 3.2). It reports results across analogy and similarity tasks and acknowledges when baselines perform comparably or better (e.g. PPMI competitiveness and SVD sometimes worse) and discusses possible causes (lack of hyper-parameter tuning, omitted techniques) rather than overclaiming (Sec. 4.4). Some comparative limitations remain (they follow baseline hyper-parameters without retuning for ngrams, which may bias GloVe/SVD results), so comparisons are good but not fully exhaustive or fully tuned.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper clearly states the main claim (ngrams improve word representations) and presents experimental evidence (e.g., PPMI ‘uni–bi’ yields ≈8% increase on Google analogy; SGNS results discussed), and gives an explicit definition for ngram contexts (Eq.3). However, mathematical treatment is limited to restating SGNS and the ngram-context definition with no formal proofs, and experimental methodology lacks critical details (no reported statistical significance, limited hyperparameter exploration, and only default settings followed, which the authors themselves note may affect results). The efficiency/novel co-occurrence-matrix construction is claimed but not substantiated with concrete results or analysis in the provided text, so some conclusions are insufficiently supported.
